generated_headline: J&J KNEW ITS BABY POWDER CONTAINED ASBESTOS SINCE 1971 — PAID $8.9 BILLION — AND TRIED TO PUT THE TALC LIABILITY INTO A FAKE BANKRUPTCY TO PROTECT SHAREHOLDERS
executive_summary: Johnson & Johnson is one of the world's largest healthcare companies. Internal J&J documents revealed through litigation showed the company had knowledge of asbestos contamination in its talc-based baby powder dating to at least 1971 — and continued selling the product for decades. In 2019, Reuters published an investigation documenting these internal records. J&J paid $100 million to the FTC in 2022 for deceptive marketing of its surgical mesh products. Most significantly, J&J used the 'Texas Two-Step' bankruptcy maneuver — creating a subsidiary LTL Management specifically to hold talc liability, then placing it in bankruptcy — in an attempt to resolve approximately 40,000 talc/asbestos lawsuits at a discount while shielding J&J's own assets. Courts ultimately rejected this approach. J&J agreed to pay $8.9 billion to settle talc claims.

labor.summary: J&J employs approximately 152,000 people globally. No major systematic labor violations have been identified as a primary concern relative to its product liability record.

environmental.summary: J&J's pharmaceutical manufacturing generates chemical waste streams. No major standalone environmental enforcement actions have been identified as a primary concern.

political.summary: J&J's Texas Two-Step strategy — using a subsidiary bankruptcy to limit talc liability — generated significant congressional and legal scrutiny. Senators introduced legislation specifically targeting the Two-Step maneuver. J&J lobbied extensively against restrictions on its ability to use subsidiary bankruptcy for mass tort liability management.

legal.summary: 2019: Reuters investigation publishes internal J&J documents showing asbestos knowledge in talc dating to 1971. 2021: J&J creates LTL Management subsidiary, places it in Chapter 11 bankruptcy in Texas Two-Step. 2023: Third Circuit rejects LTL bankruptcy — finds it lacks good faith. 2024: J&J agrees to pay $8.9 billion over 25 years to settle approximately 40,000 talc claims — contingent on 75% plaintiff acceptance. The talc settlement remains the largest personal injury settlement in US history for asbestos-related claims.

tax.summary: J&J's pharmaceutical and medical device operations use standard international IP and transfer pricing structures. The company benefits from US R&D tax credits. The $8.9 billion settlement is paid over 25 years and is partially tax-deductible. No major criminal tax enforcement actions have been documented.

executives.summary: CEO Joaquin Duato leads J&J following the split of the consumer products business into Kenvue. No J&J executive has been criminally charged for the talc asbestos suppression. The Two-Step bankruptcy maneuver — which attempted to shield J&J's assets while limiting plaintiff recovery — was designed and executed under prior CEO Alex Gorsky's tenure.

connections.summary: J&J's Texas Two-Step strategy was mirrored by 3M (Aearo Technologies for earplug liability) — both were ultimately rejected by courts. The parallel rejection of Two-Step maneuvers in 2023-2024 represents a significant limit on the use of subsidiary bankruptcy to manage mass tort liability.

allegations.disclaimer: The following are allegations and unproven claims documented in credible sources but not adjudicated.
allegations.summary: Plaintiff attorneys have alleged that J&J's internal documents — spanning from 1971 to the 1990s — show executives were aware of asbestos contamination and made decisions to continue sales and marketing while suppressing the contamination findings. J&J has contested this characterization and disputes that its talc caused the plaintiffs' cancers. No formal public response from Johnson & Johnson to these specific allegations has been documented in credible sources as of this profile's research date. EthicalAlt allegation response type: Type 3 — no documented response found.

health_record.summary: Asbestos exposure is a documented cause of mesothelioma — a rare and aggressive cancer — and other respiratory cancers. The approximately 40,000 plaintiffs in the talc litigation allege ovarian cancer and mesothelioma from decades of talc-based baby powder use. Internal J&J documents published by Reuters showed company scientists noting asbestos presence in talc samples dating to 1971. J&J disputes causation. The $8.9 billion settlement, while not an admission of liability, is the largest personal injury settlement for asbestos-related claims in US history.

cheaper: Cornstarch-based baby powder — widely available, no asbestos-in-talc concern, comparable price
Store brand cornstarch powder — identical function, no talc
healthier: Cornstarch baby powder — no asbestos contamination risk
For dry skin: unscented cornstarch or arrowroot powder as talc alternatives
diy: J&J discontinued talc-based baby powder in the US and Canada in 2020; if you have remaining talc-based J&J powder from before 2020, the FDA recommends against use
Check any cosmetic or personal care product ingredient list for 'talc' — the FDA provides guidance on talc testing at fda.gov

timeline.event: Internal J&J documents show company scientists note asbestos in talc samples — per Reuters investigation
Reuters publishes investigation revealing internal documents; J&J stock falls; class action litigation accelerates
J&J discontinues talc baby powder in US and Canada; continues selling internationally until 2023
J&J creates LTL Management subsidiary; Texas Two-Step bankruptcy filed
Third Circuit rejects LTL bankruptcy — lacks good faith; Two-Step failed
$8.9B settlement over 25 years — largest asbestos personal injury settlement in US history

community_impact.displacement: Approximately 40,000 plaintiffs — predominantly women who used J&J talc baby powder over decades — allege ovarian cancer and mesothelioma. The Texas Two-Step attempt would have resolved their claims at a significantly lower amount while shielding J&J's full balance sheet from liability.
community_impact.price_illusion: Johnson's Baby Powder carried a brand identity of safety and gentle care — 'as gentle as Johnson's' — while internal documents from 1971 showed awareness of asbestos in the talc. The brand's safety association was its primary commercial value.
community_impact.tax_math: The $8.9 billion settlement paid over 25 years generates deductions that reduce J&J's taxable income across the payment period. The Texas Two-Step strategy — had it succeeded — would have resolved billions in liability at a fraction of the cost through a good-faith-lacking subsidiary bankruptcy.
community_impact.wealth_velocity: J&J is publicly traded with broad institutional ownership. The $8.9 billion settlement represents a cost to these shareholders. The alternative — the Texas Two-Step — would have minimized this cost at the expense of the 40,000 plaintiffs with cancer diagnoses they attribute to J&J's product.

who_benefited.how: Decades of talc baby powder revenue while internal documents showed asbestos awareness; Texas Two-Step attempted to minimize liability cost

who_paid.group: 40,000 plaintiffs with ovarian cancer and mesothelioma
who_paid.how: Cancer diagnoses attributed to decades of talc use; J&J's Texas Two-Step attempted to resolve their claims at a discount through bad-faith subsidiary bankruptcy

cost_absorption.the_gap: J&J's internal documents from 1971 showed their scientists noting asbestos in talc. They kept selling baby powder. Reuters published the documents in 2019. 40,000 people sued. J&J created a fake subsidiary, put the liability in it, and filed for bankruptcy to pay less. The court said no. J&J paid $8.9 billion. It is the largest asbestos settlement in US history. They called the product Johnson's Baby Powder.
